content.full_text:
October 12, 1990

Ms. Peggy Benardi  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
428 J Street, Suite 800  
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Confirmation of Telephone Advice

Dear Ms. Benardi:

This letter is to confirm your telephone conversation of October 4, 1990 with Christopher Micheli of our office concerning Proposition 105 disclosure requirements for campaign literature. Below are the three questions he posed to you and your corresponding advice:

1. What are the Proposition 105 disclosure requirements, if any, for a direct mail piece which is composed of one side being "yes on a certain statewide proposition" and on the other side "vote for several candidates"? The proposition and all the candidates will pay for their respective shares.

You advised that, so long as the proposition did not comprise more than 50% of the direct mail piece, no disclosure is required under section 85412. However, you advised that, if the direct mail piece is a "mass mailing," the sender identification requirement under section 84305 would apply.

2. The second question involved the same piece of campaign literature that instead of being mailed directly to voters is hand delivered to persons on the street and house-to-house. Does this literature require any Proposition 105 disclosure?

You advised that there is no difference; that is, the same "50/50" rule applies, and because the "yes on a certain proposition" is not more than 50% of the advertisement, there would be no Proposition 105 disclosure requirement.

3. The third question involved the same piece of campaign literature except it is now used as a door hanger. Your advice was there is no difference in the Proposition 105 disclaimer provided no more than 50% of the total piece is devoted to one proposition.

Ms. Peggy Benardi  
October 12, 1990  
Page two

Please confirm that the above statements set forth correctly the advice given by you. Thank you for your assistance in this advice request.

Sincerely,

JOHN H. HODGSON II

JHH:ksp  
6479.01
